concepts.topic: Describe different types of supply chain management planning.
concepts.definition: Supply chain management planning operates on three levels -- like zooming in on a map. Strategic planning sets the big picture (where to build factories, which suppliers to partner with), tactical planning fills in the route (quarterly production targets, inventory policies), and operational planning handles the turn-by-turn directions (daily scheduling, shipment tracking). Apple, for example, makes strategic decisions about supplier partnerships with Foxconn, tactical decisions about seasonal iPhone production volumes, and operational decisions about daily inventory replenishment across its global warehouses.
concepts.explanation: Think of these three planning levels as nesting dolls, each fitting inside the next. Strategic planning is the outermost doll -- it defines the supply chain's architecture over years, such as Apple's decision to diversify manufacturing from China into India and Vietnam as a risk mitigation strategy. Tactical planning is the middle doll, translating strategy into quarterly or seasonal action plans; Apple's sales and operations planning process sets iPhone production targets months before a product launch, coordinating demand forecasts with supplier capacity. Operational planning is the innermost doll, governing day-to-day execution -- monitoring shipments, adjusting warehouse staffing, and resolving logistics issues in real time. Apple's inventory turnover of over 54x per year demonstrates how tightly its operational planning is calibrated. Why it matters: without all three levels working in sync, a company either drifts without direction (missing strategy), stumbles on execution (missing tactics), or burns resources on firefighting (missing operations). The best supply chains, like Apple's, layer all three seamlessly so that a strategic vision translates into products reaching millions of customers on launch day.

sources.title: Mastering the Three Levels of Supply Chain Management
Supply Chain Planning 101: From Basics to Best Practices
Supply Chain Management Concepts

relationships.description: Static and dynamic planning are approaches applied within these planning levels.
The master production schedule is a key output of tactical planning.

concepts.definition: Static planning is like printing a road map at the start of a trip and following it regardless of traffic -- the plan is fixed until the next scheduled update. Dynamic planning is like using GPS navigation that reroutes in real time as conditions change. In ERP systems like SAP, a static plan uses regenerative MRP runs on a set schedule, while a dynamic plan uses net change calculations that update automatically whenever a new order or demand shift occurs.
concepts.explanation: Imagine two restaurants preparing for dinner service. The first (static planning) prints a prep list at 8 AM based on last week's reservations and sticks to it all day -- simple and stable, but if a party of 50 walks in unexpectedly, they scramble. The second (dynamic planning) uses a live reservation system that updates the kitchen whenever a new booking comes in, adjusting prep quantities continuously. Microsoft experienced this contrast firsthand: before adopting SAP Integrated Business Planning, its supply chain relied on static spreadsheets and manual processes that could not keep pace with scaling device production. After switching to dynamic, real-time planning tools, Microsoft's planners gained the ability to capture live data from across the business and make faster, more predictive decisions. The tradeoff is that static planning provides a stable reference point -- purchasers and production planners know exactly what the plan says and can work against it confidently. Dynamic planning offers agility but requires more computational power and disciplined master data management. Why it matters: in volatile markets with unpredictable demand (think electronics or fashion), dynamic planning prevents costly overproduction or stockouts. In stable, predictable environments, static planning's simplicity can be an advantage. Most modern supply chains use both: a static baseline plan for budgeting and coordination, overlaid with dynamic net change updates to respond to real-world fluctuations.

sources.title: Set up master plans for the deprecated planning engine
Static vs. Dynamic Supply Chains: The Path to Resilience
Static Route vs Dynamic Route Planning: A Practical Guide

relationships.description: Static and dynamic are planning approaches used within the broader types of SCM planning.
The master production schedule can be managed using either static or dynamic planning methods.

concepts.topic: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.
concepts.definition: A master production schedule (MPS) is the supply chain's detailed game plan -- like a concert setlist that specifies exactly which songs (products) will be played (manufactured), in what order (sequence), and at what time (dates and quantities). It bridges demand forecasts and detailed production planning. Toyota's MPS, for example, maps every vehicle to a specific production slot with a planned line-off time, then triggers just-in-time parts orders to suppliers so components arrive precisely when needed on the assembly line.
concepts.explanation: Think of the MPS as a wedding planner's master timeline. Just as a wedding planner coordinates the caterer, florist, DJ, and venue around a single schedule, the MPS coordinates raw materials, production capacity, and customer orders around a single build plan. Determining the MPS starts with inputs: demand forecasts, the sales and operations plan, existing customer orders (the backlog), current inventory levels, material availability, and capacity constraints. These inputs are synthesized into a schedule for individual finished products expressed in specific configurations, quantities, and dates. Toyota exemplifies MPS excellence through its four-stage scheduling process. Each production day is planned down to the minute -- the first vehicle's line-off time might be 06:01 and the last at 02:00 the next morning. This precise MPS feeds directly into Toyota's Heijunka (level scheduling) system, which ensures a balanced workflow and triggers just-in-time parts deliveries from hundreds of suppliers. Time fences are a critical MPS concept -- they create zones of schedule stability. Inside the near-term fence, changes are frozen to protect production flow; outside it, adjustments are allowed to accommodate shifting demand. The planning horizon must extend at least as far as the longest cumulative lead time for any component. Why it matters: the MPS drives material requirements planning (MRP), so errors here cascade through the entire supply chain. An inaccurate MPS leads to either excess inventory (wasted capital) or stockouts (missed sales). Toyota's disciplined MPS process is a key reason it weathered the global chip shortage better than competitors -- its schedule stability allowed for earlier detection of supply disruptions.

relationships.description: The MPS is a key output of tactical-level supply chain planning.
The MPS uses the bill of materials to determine component and material requirements.
The MPS drives raw material procurement through material requirements planning.

concepts.definition: A bill of materials (BOM) is the complete recipe for a product -- a structured list of every raw material, component, and sub-assembly needed to manufacture a finished good, along with exact quantities. Like a recipe that lists '2 cups flour, 3 eggs,' a BOM for a Tesla Model 3 specifies thousands of parts from battery cells to seat bolts. Boeing manages multi-level BOMs so complex that supplier-specific BOM reports are generated to coordinate sub-assemblies built across dozens of global facilities before final aircraft assembly.
concepts.explanation: Imagine building IKEA furniture without the parts list -- you would not know if pieces were missing until halfway through assembly. A BOM prevents that chaos at industrial scale. A single-level BOM lists only the immediate components (like a furniture parts list showing 'four legs, one tabletop, eight screws'), while a multi-level or indented BOM reveals the full hierarchy, showing that each leg itself requires a wooden dowel, a metal bracket, and two rubber feet. In practice, companies maintain different BOM views. Boeing uses engineering BOMs (EBOMs) during aircraft design in CAD software, then converts them into manufacturing BOMs (MBOMs) that guide the actual assembly process. Each Boeing 787 Dreamliner has a BOM spanning tens of thousands of parts sourced from suppliers in over 40 countries -- supplier-specific BOM export reports tell each partner exactly which sub-assemblies to build and ship. Tesla's early struggles illustrate why BOM accuracy is critical: in 2008, when Elon Musk asked 'How much does it cost to build a car?', the company could not answer because its BOM data was unreliable, contributing to Roadster cost overruns that nearly bankrupted the company. Why it matters: the BOM is the foundation of material requirements planning (MRP). When the master production schedule is 'exploded' through the BOM, it generates precise purchasing and production orders for every component. An inaccurate BOM means wrong quantities ordered, production delays, and cost overruns rippling across the supply chain.

sources.title: Bills of Material (BOM) In the Supply Chain

relationships.description: The BOM is used to explode the MPS into detailed material requirements.
The BOM specifies the raw materials needed for production.
Sub-assemblies listed in a BOM become work-in-process during manufacturing.

concepts.topic: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.
concepts.definition: Raw materials are the unprocessed or minimally processed inputs -- the foundational ingredients like steel, semiconductors, plastics, and agricultural products -- that feed the production process and represent the first link in the supply chain. Think of them as the flour and eggs before baking begins. Toyota classifies raw materials as direct (steel panels that become car bodies) or indirect (lubricants for assembly robots), and famously stockpiles roughly 1,500 critical components to buffer against supply disruptions like the global semiconductor shortage.
concepts.explanation: Raw materials procurement is like stocking a kitchen before cooking season -- if you run out of a key ingredient, everything downstream stops. This played out dramatically during the 2021 global semiconductor shortage. Most automakers were caught flat-footed, but Toyota fared better because it had learned from the 2011 Fukushima earthquake to maintain safety stock of critical raw materials and components. Toyota created a list of approximately 1,500 parts it deemed essential to stockpile, and built an intricate early-warning system to monitor not just its direct suppliers but the smaller companies those suppliers buy from -- sometimes four or five tiers deep. Raw materials fall into two categories: direct materials that physically become part of the finished product (silicon wafers in a chip, cotton in a shirt, steel in a car frame) and indirect materials that support production without entering the product (machine lubricants, cleaning solvents, protective packaging). Effective raw materials inventory management involves a balancing act: too little stock risks production shutdowns when supplies are disrupted (as many automakers experienced, losing billions in revenue during the chip shortage), while too much stock ties up working capital and warehouse space. Why it matters: raw materials sit at the very start of the production chain, so disruptions here cascade through every downstream stage. Companies that invest in supplier visibility, dual-sourcing strategies, and strategic safety stock -- as Toyota demonstrated -- gain a significant competitive advantage when supply shocks hit.

sources.title: What Are Raw Materials?
Raw Materials Inventory: Explanation, Benefits, and Tips
Raw Material - Definition, Importance, Types & Example

relationships.description: The BOM specifies which raw materials are required and in what quantities.
Raw materials become work-in-process once they enter the production process.
Raw materials are transformed through production into finished goods.

concepts.topic: Describe work-in-process for supply chain management.
concepts.definition: Work-in-process (WIP) refers to partially completed goods caught between raw materials and finished products -- like dough that has been mixed and shaped but not yet baked. These items have consumed labor, materials, and overhead but cannot be sold yet. Toyota's lean production system treats excess WIP as a form of waste, using Kanban cards as visual signals to limit WIP on the factory floor, ensuring each station pulls only what the next step needs rather than pushing excess inventory forward.
concepts.explanation: Picture a highway during rush hour. Every car stuck in traffic is 'work-in-process' -- fuel and time have been invested, but no one has reached their destination yet. The more cars jammed on the road (high WIP), the slower everything moves; fewer cars (low WIP) means smoother flow and faster arrival. This is exactly the insight behind Toyota's Production System. Taiichi Ohno, the architect of Toyota's lean manufacturing, observed that WIP inventory is essentially money frozen on the factory floor -- materials, labor, and overhead have been spent, but no revenue is generated until the product is complete and shipped. Toyota's solution was the Kanban system: physical cards (now often digital signals) that authorize production only when the downstream station is ready, creating a 'pull' system that naturally limits WIP. The result is shorter lead times, fewer bottlenecks, and lower carrying costs. On the balance sheet, WIP appears as a current asset under inventory, calculated as beginning WIP plus manufacturing costs incurred minus cost of goods completed. High WIP levels are a red flag -- they signal bottlenecks, quality problems, or poor scheduling. Boeing, managing aircraft assembly where a single plane can spend months in WIP, uses advanced inventory synchronization systems to coordinate thousands of suppliers and keep partially assembled aircraft moving through production stages without stalling. Why it matters: WIP directly affects lead times, cash flow, and production agility. Companies that minimize WIP -- through lean techniques, better scheduling, or improved quality -- free up capital, respond faster to demand changes, and reduce the risk of obsolete inventory sitting on the factory floor.

sources.title: Work in process
The Monthly Metric: Work-in-Process Inventory
What is Work in Process?

relationships.description: Raw materials become work-in-process once they enter manufacturing.
Work-in-process becomes finished goods upon completion of manufacturing.
The master production schedule drives the flow of WIP through the production process.

concepts.topic: Define finished goods and the goal to move those goods.
concepts.definition: Finished goods are products that have completed the entire manufacturing process, passed quality inspection, and are ready for sale -- like a meal that has been plated and is waiting for the server to deliver it. The primary goal is to move these goods to customers as quickly and efficiently as possible. Zara exemplifies this: its centralized distribution hub in Arteixo, Spain processes finished garments and delivers them to any store worldwide within 24-48 hours in Europe and 48-72 hours globally, using cross-docking to avoid warehouse storage entirely.
concepts.explanation: Think of finished goods inventory as perishable fruit on a grocery shelf -- the longer it sits, the more value it loses through holding costs, obsolescence risk, and tied-up capital. The goal is to get products from the factory floor to the customer's hands as fast as possible, and few companies do this better than Zara. Zara's parent company Inditex operates a remarkably fast finished goods pipeline: stores place orders twice per week on fixed schedules, and garments arrive already labeled and priced, ready for the sales floor. Zara uses cross-docking at its distribution center, meaning incoming finished goods are transferred directly from receiving to outbound shipping without being stored in between -- minimizing the time products spend as idle inventory. This speed-to-market approach lets Zara produce up to 1,000 new designs per month and get them into stores while trends are still fresh, rather than forecasting months in advance and risking unsold stock. Finished goods inventory serves as a buffer between production output and customer demand. Too little inventory means stockouts and lost sales during demand surges; too much means warehouses full of products losing value. On the balance sheet, finished goods are classified as current assets, and the carrying cost includes warehousing, insurance, depreciation, and opportunity cost of capital. Why it matters: finished goods represent the final stage where all supply chain investment -- raw materials, labor, overhead -- is either converted into revenue or trapped as excess stock. Effective finished goods management through fast distribution, demand-responsive replenishment, and lean inventory practices directly determines customer satisfaction, order fulfillment rates, and ultimately profitability.

sources.title: Finished Goods Inventory: What It Is, Formula, and Examples

relationships.description: Work-in-process becomes finished goods upon completion of manufacturing.
Finished goods are the end result of transforming raw materials through production.
The master production schedule determines what finished goods will be produced and when.
